content:
Artificial Intelligence

Software Development

Generative Ai

Ai Coding

Developer Productivity

6 min read
Just now

--

Part 6 of the DevFlow series — the last experiment. Part 5 held the stack constant and varied the AI tools. This time I did the opposite: same intent, and everything technical gets replaced.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Part 5 ended with a claim I hadn’t yet earned: if the artifacts capture a behavioral contract rather than an implementation binding, then the implementation underneath should be swappable.
Time to earn it.
The setup: take the same frozen DevFlow artifacts that drove the Quiz Bank builds — intent, interview, objectives, tasks — and rebuild the application on a completely different technology stack. Not a gentle migration. Everything goes:

• Backend: Python + Flask → Java 21 + Spring Boot 3

• Frontend: Vue 3 + TypeScript → React 18 + TypeScript

• Database: SQLite (a file in the repo) → PostgreSQL + Flyway (a running service)
Different language. Different framework. Different persistence model — one is a file in the repo, the other is a running service with real migrations. If anything was going to expose intent that had secretly fused with implementation, this would.
The question: when you replace every technical decision, what survives?

What Survived: The Entire Behavioral Contract
Both stacks shipped. Both were built, started, and verified in-browser — every flow, end to end. And against the same 18 tasks:

• All 87 acceptance criteria: PASS on both stacks. Same count, behaviorally identical — only the technical wording differs (./mvnw spring-boot:run where the other says py -m backend).

• Same three user-visible flows, same validation rules, same minimum-3-questions gate, same per-question auto-save, same results page semantics.

• No scope creep in either direction. Neither build added features the artifacts didn’t ask for.

• Even the logical schema survived: same table names, same column names (correct, option_a..d), same 0-based answer index, same difficulty default — while the physical layer diverged completely (PRAGMA-versioned SQLite file vs. Flyway-migrated PostgreSQL with enforced foreign keys).
Hold that last one. Two databases that couldn’t be more different operationally, agreeing on every logical name — that’s not luck, and I’ll come back to why.
Meanwhile the implementations themselves look nothing alike: 13 Python files vs. 26 Java files, ORM vs. raw JdbcTemplate, and — my favorite detail — the Java build is smaller (6.5k vs. 8.1k lines) despite having twice the file count. Structure diverged everywhere the behavior didn’t.
Part 5’s finding, one level up: behavior converges where artifacts speak; structure diverges where they’re silent — even when you change the entire language underneath.

The Anatomy: Where Technology Actually Lives in a Spec
Here’s the part I genuinely didn’t know before running this: when a stack change hits an artifact chain, where exactly does it land?
Because the original artifacts weren’t stack-neutral. The interview had confirmed Python, SQLite, and a plain-JS frontend as explicit user answers. The objectives carried a Tech Stack constraint row. The tasks were full of py -m backend and frontend/js/app.js. Swapping stacks meant finding every place the old technology was pinned — which turned into a checklist, and the checklist turned into the experiment's most interesting artifact.
The damage map, by layer:

• Intent — nothing. Zero edits. The why of the project contains no technology.

• Interview — question structure survived; three answers superseded (backend, frontend, database). The uncertainty-removal logic didn’t change — only the choices made.

• Objective — scope, success criteria, and product boundaries untouched. Two rows (Tech Stack, Platform) and some startup wording: replaced.

• Tasks — heavy rewrite. Commands, file paths, framework idioms all rebound.

• UI spec & product scope — untouched.
Read that top to bottom and you’re looking at a reuse gradient: the closer an artifact sits to why and what, the more it survives a technology change; the closer it sits to how, the more it’s consumable. Intent is permanent. Tasks are ammunition.
And the pins were enumerable. Technology didn’t turn out to be smeared through the spec like dye through water — it sat in a countable set of locations: three interview answers, two objective rows, and the command/path layer of tasks. A stack refactor stopped being archaeology and became a checklist pass: align the markdown first, implement second.
This is the payoff Part 1 was reaching for. The PoC-to-MVP drift problem — “the original development context becomes impossible to reconstruct” — dissolves when the context is files with a known gradient. The requirement layers are reusable building blocks. Rebuild? The blocks are already there. New stack constraints at MVP time? You now know exactly which rows change.
Intent is the asset. Implementation is the consumable.

The Loop Closes: Last Experiment’s Failures, This Experiment’s Rules
One more thing happened here, and it’s quietly my favorite result of the whole series.
Part 5’s rebuild experiment produced two failures:

• One build’s evidence was all-PASS while its UI was dead on arrival — unit tests green, real app broken.

• One build renamed a database column nothing had pinned down — perfectly reasonable, and it broke shared-data compatibility.
Both failures went into the stack-swap checklist as rules, before this experiment ran:

• A branch is not complete without at least one black-box smoke check against the running app — because an all-green unit suite already shipped a broken UI once.

• Cross-stack branches must preserve the shared behavior contract: routes, payload shapes, and logical schema names — because an unpinned column name already cost us once.
(Plus a third, smaller one: inherited task files carried Status: verified checkboxes from the original build, and a fresh session once treated unstarted work as done. New rule: reset statuses on every rebuild branch.)
Result this round: zero hidden bugs. Both stacks browser-verified. Logical schema held across SQLite and PostgreSQL. That schema agreement I told you to hold onto? Not luck — it was last experiment’s divergence, promoted to this experiment’s constraint.
That’s the loop Part 5 promised, actually closing: divergence report → new constraints → clean run. Constraints in this system aren’t designed in advance by someone wise. They’re harvested from failures, written into artifacts, and verified by the next iteration. The spec gets smarter every time something goes wrong — because something went wrong, and the going-wrong was recorded somewhere durable enough to learn from.
Chat history can’t do that. Files can.

What Six Parts Add Up To
The series in one breath:
AI sessions forget (Part 1) because degradation is structural (Part 2). So move the work into artifacts (Part 3), verify at cheap checkpoints instead of trusting at expensive edges (Part 4). Do that, and the same spec survives a change of AI tools (Part 5) — and now, a change of the entire technology stack (Part 6). Along the way, the failures themselves became constraints, and the constraints became the reason the last experiment ran clean.
The claims are no longer claims. The branches are public:

• Repo: devflow-example-quiz-bank

• rebuild/* — one spec, three AI tools (Part 5)

• refactor/* — one intent, two full stacks (this part), with the alignment checklist at docs/tech-stack.md on refactor/base
Diff them. Check whether the survival map matches what I’ve told you.
There’s one article left in this series, and it’s not another experiment. It’s an honest accounting of why DevFlow looks the way it does. The short version: one principle — never trust an AI-assisted step you can’t verify — decided that everything gets written down; a set of engineering scars from Part 1 decided what shape the files would take. The closing piece tells both stories, and shows the structure the artifact chain was hiding all along: not five documents in a row, but three verification loops.

Your current project: if you had to rebuild it on a different stack next quarter, what would survive? If the honest answer is “whatever’s in my head” — that’s the gap.
